title: 2025/12/04 - PP (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Primary Production Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Primary Production Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries Hearing of evidence: Ministry for Primary Industries Ray Smith, Director-General MPI Gillon Carruthers, DDG Public Affairs Dan Bolger, DDG Fisheries New Zealand Sam Keenan, DDG Te Uru Rakau – New Zealand Forest Service Stuart Anderson, DDG Biosecurity New Zealand Karen Adair, DDG Agriculture and Investment Services Vince Arbuckle, DDG New Zealand Food Safety Julie Collins, DDG Policy & Trade Neil Cherry, DDG Corporate Services Glenn Webber, DDG Māori Partnerships and Investments John Roche, Chief Science Adviser Agriculture /Animal Welfare Diana Reaich, Associate DDG Trade & Intl Relations Phil Houlding, Chief Trade Policy Officer Jane Chirnside, Director Resources and Rural Communities Steve Ainsworth, Div Mgr Bilateral Relations & Trade Robyn Meehan, Director Māori Partnership and Capability Glen Burrell, Director Compliance & Response Fiona Duncan, Director Regulatory Systems Policy Alastair Cameron, Director Primary Sector Policy Steve Penno, Director Investment Programmes & Ops Biosecurity Mary van Andel, Chief Veterinary Officer John Walsh, Director Pest Management Michael Inglis, Commissioner North Diane McDermott, Commissioner Central/South Andrew Spelman, Commissioner Biosecurity Intelligence Fisheries Emma Taylor, Director Fisheries Management Simon Lawrence, Director Science & Information Steve Ham, Director Fisheries Compliance Alastair Cameron, Director Primary Sector Policy Charlotte Denny, Div Mgr Sustainable Trade & Environment Forestry Olivia Sullivan, Director Forestry System Alastair Cameron, Director Primary Sector Policy Corporate Services Ranjit Singh, Financial Controller Bruce Arnold, Director Cost Recovery Brent Healy, Chief Financial Officer Kaye Ryan, Director People & Capability
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries. Ministry for Primary Industries. Ray Smith, Director-General MPI

chunks.text: I guess- You just gave us nice (audio cuts off)

We've got a few reserves in case- (laughs) Yeah, yeah.... in case you get too tough on us. (laughs) Fair enough. (laughs) Got the entire World Cup squad coming to the room full at once. (laughs) Oh, yeah, what do you think of the draw? Has anyone- Uh, it always sucks. You have to-

Ask them What team's the best one? You have to pick. We have to be the best team to win anyway so, don't record it. Oh, yeah. Nice uh... Very good. Yeah. Oh, right. Sit. (laughs) Well, welcome to the, um... (clears throat)... Primary Production Select Committee annual review.

Um, got you for most of the day, Ray, so you'll be thrilled about that. And... (laughs) Spending time in our company. (laughs) So, um, w- we'll start off with your introductory remarks and then- Sure. ... um, sort of got a, um, structured agenda- Yep.

... that we'll w- work through. I think you've probably been, it's been shared with you I think? Yup, yup, got that. So, um, we'll try and stick to that as best we can. We'll be flexible. (laughs) Thank you, so will we. So, um, leave it in your hands. Okay, well, thanks very much. Um, look, and can I start by thanking the committee for your work across the last year.

Um, it's, it's a very, you're a very active committee, and, um, I think we all together attend a lot of events together (laughs) . Um, and just to say, um, we appreciate it as well because it makes a big difference, you know, whether it's things that are happening on the ground or whether it's the kind of formal events that we all go to, um, we realize just the, the commitment on your part as well to supporting the primary sector, uh, and maintaining those relationships.

Um, uh, look, we've got a pretty strong team here. Obviously, we cover a lot of ground, um, so, we'll, we'll just, we'll just bring people and where we kind of get into areas of expertise where that's needed. Um, I know you've heard from some of our ministers already this week, um, and- (clears throat)... there's a bit more to come on that, uh, I think tomorrow. But, uh, it's worth repeating that the primary sector is in pretty good health. Um, exports of food and fiber products, um, remain strong despite, uh, some trade turbulence.

And we will release our situation outlook for primary industries in the next week or two. And, um, you know, I expect us to build on the excellent, excellent result we had, um, last time. So, look, I'm really proud- (clears throat)... of the work that the sector has done across the last year.... happens in a lot of ways, um, through supporting the work of catchment groups on the ground, backing research to help reduce emissions, supporting farms and growers to recover from adverse events, uh, market access work of course, and

by maintaining some of the strongest biosecurity measures, uh, in the world. And of course, not forgetting the sustainability of our seafood sector and, and of course our important forestry sector. Uh, as part of our focus on growing, uh, New Zealand's economy, um, in the last two months, I have visited five of our top six trading nations. Um, so I've been in Australia, United States, Japan, Korea, and China, um, with a focus on maintaining, um, the good relationships that we have there and opening up market access opportunities wherever we can.

Um, and we have good access in overseas markets, um, but there are also opportunities for investment back into New Zealand. And some of the conversation around growing our agriculture sector, uh, is a real opportunity and one where we're, we are, are talking about that internationally in order to try and bring, uh, interest and investment, uh, for those very high value salmon, mussel and oyster products to see if we can grow that sector out.

Um, certainly for me, um, our reputation's strong internationally. People like our products, they trust where they come from, uh, and how we produce them. Uh, and we're in a pretty, uh, positive position, albeit that there's a lot of competition, uh, particularly from our, our cousins across, across the ditch. I also got to visit some of the cutting edge sites and the investments that we have in methane mitigating technologies, uh, while I was in the United States. I particularly wanted to see that, um, and happy to talk

about, uh, what that was looking like and what I think the opportunities might be there. And in Japan, it was good to be able to visit the, you know... New Zealand imports a large amount of, uh, secondhand vehicles from Japan, just so happens they drive on the same side of the road as us. So, um, that's, that's a great export, uh, to New Zealand. But all of those go through treatment facilities before they come to New Zealand for biosecurity.

And I was able to go through that facility myself and have a look at how they do it. Actually found a stink bug on the floor that had dropped out of the heat treatment chambers, so they do work. Um, it's quite an important part of the operation. Obviously those cars are checked again when they arrive in New Zealand, but it's a, it's a, it's quite a big part of the operation, so it was good to be able to see that. Um, we made a big investment on this last year in getting, uh, lifting our plant health and environment laboratory.

And I'd encourage you if you get a chance when you're in Auckland, take a look. It's next to the pla- uh, the old plant and food laboratory. Um, it's coming out of the ground quite quickly. It's on, it's on track and it will give us, um, the capability as the years go by to be able to manage any large incursions that might affect our, our, our, um, our plant food industry, uh, or our forestry industry. But also it's increasing the capacity for us to bring in new varietals and things, which has always been a bit of a bottleneck, uh, to

bring those. If you bring a new varietal into the country, it might be a prunus type thing, it can be a couple of years in a quarantine facility before we can be certain that there are no viruses or diseases that are present. It's always a bit challenging that area, but we're, we're, we're growing out the capacity. Our, our facility in Tamaki that looks after plant health is end of life. Um, and some of those pa- some of the parts of that facility have started to break down. So we're right on track for getting a new facility 2029, uh, that's

happening now. So we were pretty pleased to get that investment. It's a substantial investment. Um, it's, it's about, um, 600 odd million, um, for the development of that. But it has a 50 year lifespan and that will give us alongside our animal and plant, our animal health facility out at Wallsville, uh, the one in, the one in Auckland will cover off both sides, uh, of the equation. So we'll have animal health and plant health, um, with facilities that guarantee, uh, we can do the things we need to do in New Zealand.

Um, look, there's obviously been some, uh, challenges, some ups and downs with weather and other adverse events across the last year, whether it was kind of not enough rain or too much, uh, or too much wind and those types of things. There's, there's always been challenges there. Uh, and of course we successfully dealt with the outbreaks of bird flu in Otago, although it had a bit of a tail on it in terms of, uh, trade related matters across the past, uh, year, which we've managed to largely, uh, restore. We had two fruit fly incursions,

um, uh, last summer. Uh, so we were quite busy through that Christmas period and of course our team's pretty busy with the hornet at the moment, uh, in Auckland. So, um, just a bit of an update on the hornet, um, Stu will be able to give you a bit more on it later, but, um, we've caught 31 queens. Um, so mo- 20 of those have nests or there's evidence they're nesting.

Um, uh, so that's, that's been good. Most of these catches have been through visual inspections- Mm-hmm.... um, rather than reported, um, sightings. Four and a half thousand notifications. So we've kind of whittled that down to the ones that we've f- found that are actually accurate. And over 2,200 houses visited, um, in that, um, uh, affected area.

We're starting a leaflet drop at the moment, which will go to a hundred thousand houses in that North Shore area and that we've extended the buffer zone out to five Ks. And, uh, we are just wanting to make sure if we can just get every householder having a bit of a look around the eaves and the decks and you know, if you've got a wood pile or, um, some particularly, um, uh, interesting trees and so on in your backyard, if you can find these little golf ball or tennis size ball, um, nests and let us know. And that's gonna be our best way, uh, of being able to, of being

able to kind of close this down. Um, so look, we're in a, we've, we've started well. Um, it's not, it's not, it's not cheap either. And so just to give you a bit of a sense, you know, we've probably spent around about a million dollars already on this response. Um, uh, and we have to be conscious of following the science in order to kind of spend that money wisely as we go forward.

'Cause each month tick by, ticks by, if you're adding on at roughly a million dollars a month, uh, in response costs with a lot of people on the ground, it starts to become very expensive. So we just need to make sure that, um, we're spending the money upfront, um, in order to try and close this down. But, you know, we will have to keep making choices about how far we go and how much we do along the way. So that'll, might be a point of interest for the committee along the course of next year as well, depending on whether we can close it out

early or whether this kind of starts to, to take a slightly longer course. Um, probably, um-... uh, one other thing I did, a couple of things I wanted to mention. M. bovis, um, M- Minister Hogg had mentioned it, uh, earlier this week. Uh, this, this is a su- this is really a subst- really substantive point in our progress on this programme.

And we have spent nearly $800 million on it so far of the $860 million, uh, that was, that was voted for it. Um, we always thought it was a 10-year programme. It's likely that we'll be able to conclude it a year earlier than that. So, where we are right now, uh, sort of seven to, to eight years into the programme, um, we, we haven't seen the disease for two years, so we've, we've been able to move to this, um, confidence phase of absence of the disease.

If we get through one more spring season, autumn and spring calving season, so this time next year, if we don't see the disease- (clears throat)... turn up, we think a year earlier than anticipated, we'd be able to declare freedom from the disease. Excellent. It's substantial because so many people were affected. Um, hundreds of farmers, uh, who lost, um, who had their cows culled. Many other, probably thousands of farmers who had notices of direction at various points throughout the process as we tried to understand what was

happening with their herds. Uh, and, you know, people have been, um, incredibly good through that. It's not been easy. I mean, we were a bit bumpy at the start, as you can imagine. Um, we had nobody dedicated to this and then we had 450 staff on it, uh, for a long period. And, you know, finding the expertise, the capability, and building our systems. We have learnt a lot as a result of d- undertaking this.

A, in terms of, uh, how to ma- how to help support people and to get in early with those supports. And, and B, to have the capability to know how to respond to these animal diseases. It's the biggest one we've tried to get in front of, uh, in the cattle sector, um, short of TB, which kind of really kicked off, uh, big time in the '70s and '80s. So, um, we're almost there.

Uh, we're not home yet. Um, we should feel proud about it. And, you know, what it means for New Zealand, uh, New Zealand, New Zealand farming systems move their animals around more than almost any other in the world. Mm-hmm. That's how we're so productive. And so, uh, if you're moving animals around a lot, you, you run the risk of spreading disease quite rapidly. So, to maintain the farming system we had, removing this disease was important to New Zealand. Uh, we are only one of two countries in the world that doesn't have

the disease. Uh, Norway being the other one. Uh, but avoiding lameness, mastitis, uh, and aborted calves, and, uh, farmers having to be much more discerning about where their animals come from in the future, uh, is something we've managed to avoid. So, uh, almost there. Uh, and just did want to acknowledge that a huge amount of work has been done in that area. Um, last few points. Um, we have 121 performance measures. Um, it's a big department.

Um, not so much, uh, you know, it's 3,250 staff. It's not so much the size staffing-wise, but the breadth of what we cover. Uh, we've achieved 107 of those 121 performance measures, about 88%. So, we were about 89% last year, so we're roughly in the same place. Um, but I would note that seven of them weren't active, i.e.

we didn't have to activate those measures because we didn't have to administer adverse events funds and things like that, uh, in particular, well, particular programmes to h- of recovery. Uh, so we were probably a little bit better than, than we showed on the performance there. But that's, that's all detailed out. And I did want to acknowledge that, that we've taken a pretty strong disciplined approach to our financial management across the last year or two in particular.

Um, we were able to increase our capital investment, uh, in MPI's critical systems and assets. So sometimes these things don't get seen, but they're actually really important. If we're managing the place well, we can keep investing. Um, so that Plant Health Laboratory is a big investment, um, ahead of getting the government's investment, um, uh, capital investment, we, we put in nearly $20 million of our own investment to get to the point of having a business case that would support that development.

Um, digitizing the trade certification. So this is kind of getting, if you think about export certificates for all of the products that leave New Zealand, moving from paper to digitization. And with China, uh, we've just agreed to go further with that to almost get to 100%. Um, so we are leading with them in the world at digitizing trade. Uh, our upgrade of the ETS forestry system and, um, the Cameras on Boats programme, uh, there's been a big investment.

Uh, opening of, well, I guess I'd say they're not new, but, um, two international airports reopened, Hamilton and Dunedin. And so we had- Oh. ... to staff those, uh, made sure the facilities were okay. So Dunedin was a bit of a refresh. Hamilton, we had to, uh, work with the airport companies and get the investment right and so on. And I think you've had some of those folks through here. Uh, a new mail center was opened with New Zealand Post and Customs.

(clears throat) That's got new technology in it. That technology, uh, is quite amazing. Worth having a look at if you, if you want to have a, have a, a view of that. Just seeing the, um, 3D technology that we're able to use for looking at parcels and things to see what's inside them. So we're in a much stronger position there. And some of that technology- (clears throat)... we want to invest in, in the airport infrastructure going forward with some of those machines are now kind of getting towards the end of their useful

life. And, um, we did reduce our consultant and contractor ing- expenditure by $25 million. So we're down from $40 to $15 million and that's, uh- (clears throat) ... also given us quite a bit more headroom. We were able to carry forward $16 million in underspends, uh, to invest in biosecurity responses, which we're really grateful to be able to do that. And I guess we've been able to present ourselves in a way that says, uh, we can make savings within our boat. Um, we can operate it, uh, under the cost and we've been given the opportunity to transfer some of that money

into priorities that, um, a lo- a lot of people, um, see as being things that we need to keep investing in. So, um, I'll probably leave it there and, um, just, uh, thank my team for what's been a, a pretty good year. Yeah. Anyways. Y- That's true. So, um, I know Sam's got a question about the, um, biosecurity f- facility in Tamaki, so.

Mm-hmm. Yes, That one first. Joe? The... Thank you, Mr. Chair. Uh, thank you for your introduction there and all the work that you and all of the team do. Kia ora. Um, we had, uh, some plant importers, uh, in here recently. And they were talking about the proposed facility in Mount Albert and, um, saying that it's, it's very expensive and it's probably based on, uh, outdated technologies. Technologies have moved a significant amount, um, since it was, it was first concepted....

and that, um, they feel that the industry hasn't been properly consulted. It's expensive. Obviously, the government's in a tight fiscal situation. Um, are you confident that that spend of what will be- Yeah.... 500 mil, potentially more- Yeah.

... is what the, what the market needs and also- Yeah.... will serve the country well given- Yeah. ... given the significant cost? You know, g- uh, really good question. Y- yes, I am. And we actually consulted widely on this. Um, uh, I'm aware of the sector that, that and, and the individuals, um, 'cause they raise those matters with me regularly. Uh, there is an increase in cost and th- when I started this job seven years ago, uh, one of the big things that, uh, I was confronted with was a large amount of complaint about the fact people couldn't bring products into

the country and there was a long queue because we didn't have enough facilities to bring them in to quarantine. So I've set about trying to fix that. It's taken years to get to a point where we got the investment, where we completed the business case. That business case has been through multiple iterations, uh, and through cabinet. Uh, we've had to prove that, um, this is the right thing to do. And, um, and I'm grateful that we're on the way.

The Tamaki facility that we have, you know, for example, fortunately, we'd made some early investment in new, um, glasshouse facilities because, uh, we'd run into a problem in our Tamaki facility in terms of the... When you have these, um, facilities, you're trying to make sure that whatever you're testing, nothing can escape from the facility into the wider environment. And we found we've got a problem in our Tamaki facility, we already had to shift everything out of there into these glasshouses that we've kind of already got ahead

of schedule. Um, so one, one of the things that, um, that's really important, uh, for New Zealanders to realize is that we are... So much of our economy is dependent on, um, you know, the biologicals, if you like. Um, you know, we're largely, you know, our growth comes off, uh, growing protein and growing fruit, uh, vegetables, and our forestry sector. And, and for the size of those, we really need to be able to have a facility that's at the state-of-the-art level where we can, we can test products.

If we got another, if we got another PSA type outbreak that we had with the kiwifruit, um, virus, you want to know that we can do all the sampling, testing and management of that in New Zealand, not send the samples to Australia. And a really good example of that was with the bird flu outbreak we had in Dunedin. Because we had rebuilt the lab at Wallaceville, we were able to do all the sampling and testing in New Zealand, we didn't have to send it to Australia and get into a long queue of waiting to see what happens. So it's the same on the plant health side.

That's the other big side of the economy. And if we get a bark beetle in a forest, uh, or we get something, uh, something, uh, within our, um, I don't know, we could get a virus in the tomato sector or in the potato sector or in the onions, we wanna be able to test those things and check them out. The importation, the importation side of it's actually the smaller part.

It's about 10% of the total value of that facility. The big thing is having a laboratory where our people can contain viruses and diseases and not allow them to escape, um, and be able to do their important work here in New Zealand. So, what we do is we underpin, um, we underpin this $60 billion of export earnings, uh, by having an animal facility that's, uh, it, it has the, the most capable laboratories in the country, and at the other end of the country we have a plant health facility that can do the same thing.

Right. So that covers off both sectors. There is a... You're right though, there is a cost implication. And, you know, everybody wants, everybody wants the services, um, nobody's that keen on paying a bit more to get them. And w- and the charge out rates for using the plant health laboratory, we have increased them. I mean, essentially the state was subsidizing it at about 95% of the cost, and we shifted that to 50/50.

Um, just on that cost. And, and you're saying, you're saying a few projects under this government- Yep.... um, they, they looked at and, and the- Yep.... alternatives put forward. Um, this is a big project, right? Yep. 500 mil, potentially more. Yep. Um, is, is that, is the, the size and scope of the project necessary or, or is there a way that it could be done in a more fiscally constrained manner?

No. It's a- it's a- it's absolutely necessary, um, because, because of the scale of what we're protecting. So, um, unlike, unlike almost any other country in the world, New Zealand exports 95% of the food it produces. It's how we make our money. And so, um, so we don't export our surpluses. Um, there's only five million of us, and we produce enough food for, you know, 10% of 400 million people so that's- No, no. I, I get that wider picture that you're trying to frame and, and that that's not really what the questions about.

It's a, a $500 million facility. Is it... I'm seeing many examples in around the country like- Yeah. Is, is there... I mean, that's a big gold standard facility but could you do it with a- No. ... with a more toned down facility and give a couple of hundred billion back to the taxpayer? Uh, no, and we're not going to- No.... because we've already signed up to do this- Yep.

... and it's been through cabinet and it's had multiple reviews on the way through to getting there. So this is, this is, this is, uh... In order to get that level of investment through the cabinet and through the finance minister, it's had to go through multiple iterations. Oh. It's, it's come to this point. So, um, no, look, I stand, I stand by the...

Look, I have- Let this go. I just wanted some clarity No, no. W- just, but just to give a- They're good questions. To give a bit of a sense of it, I mean, I have in my career built prisons, residential facilities, laboratories. Uh, the spend on this, you know, um, the prisons that we built in my time and that would now cost one to 1.2, $1.4 billion to build. Um, this is a, this is a roughly sort of five to $600 million investment and it gives you a 50 year return. It looks about right to me. It looks about right to me for ease and lease.

Sure. But, but look at... You're right, it is a big investment, um, and, uh, we've taken it very seriously. Thank you. Steve, you got a sup? Yeah, just a sup. I mean, there's no argument on the vital importance of perfect bio-security, protecting, um, our-... productive sector here from incursions.

That's a good explanation. The need for a quarantine facility, absolutely, no question about that. I think the question arises around where technology has significantly- Yup. improved, uh, in the last decade, but partic- even in the last five years- Yup. ... where there are much lower cost means of testing germplasm and so forth, and whether there's, um, in no way, uh, compromising- Yeah. ... the strength of the biosecurity net, um, whether there are ways that some of this testing could be being done- Yeah. ... at a lower cost for the

benefit of industry. And- I, I think that will happen as well. So, I, I think, I think what's gonna happen is you're gonna get both of those things. But it's not going to replace the fact that the vast amount of work that happens every day in the laboratories, the, the, the testing of material coming into New Zealand is actually a small part of that facility. Hm. Um, it just happens to be the facility that has to do it, 'cause there are, there are some private sector capabilities, but they're not at the standard, um, is the difficulty. And so, um, yes,

AI is going to make a massive difference and it already is. And I saw that when I was in the United States, uh, but you're still gonna need laboratories. And, and every day those people are running any number of tests on, um, things that are picked up in New Zealand, uh, or that are routine, uh, in our, in our plant sector. So no, look, I, I think you're gonna need both.

But that, that will reduce the cost and potentially open up more options, I, I agree with that. Yeah. Thank you. Thank So- That's where I was heading with it. Thanks, Steve. It's comp- (coughs) It's complementary, not, not a replacement of. Yeah, I've got a final sup on that. So, new facility in conjunction with new technology that's gonna be coming along, what's the likelihood of being able to speed up the process so that, um, new cultivars or, or- Mm-hmm. ... germplasm isn't stuck in limbo for- Yeah. ...

you know, years? Mm-hmm. Yeah, and, um, uh, the biosecurity team have been out consulting quite widely on how to do that. 'Cause we agree that, uh, the process could be better. Mm-hmm. Um, it's... If you sort of, how did we get here? And this is the difficulty with biosecurity. We face it with the hornets. People would like us to do almost everything from Invercargill, you know, to Cape Reinga.

Um, so when these things arrive, people become seized by it and they expect us to resolve it. In order to try and stop these things from happening and to invest in them really, pe- nobody really wants to do that. But when they arrive, they expect the services to be there. Mm-hmm. So, um, w- when I think about, um, this area, a lot, you know, if you go back to 2010, New Zealand's kiwi, kiwifruit industry was almost wiped out. Mm-hmm.

Uh, we were lucky we had another cultivar that they had invested in, um, through Plant & Food that, uh, so the goal, you know, came to the fore. Uh, a lot of people were hurt and damaged in that, uh, and many came out better out the other end. We still had PSA, um, but, and we also, we settled, I settled it, $50 million, uh, to conclude the, uh, court case on that that had been in the, in the Supre- uh, heading to the Supreme Court.

So the cost of that was vast, um, and the risk of it was huge. And so, so much of the import health standard, um, development beyond that became much more risk averse, um, because we couldn't have that happen again. Um, and so it did tighten the procedures and it did slow things down, and what the team are trying to do now is say, "Okay, we know more, we're in a better place, how do we start to make sure that we're not being overly restrictive, that we're holding things for too long?" The challenge in all of this is when you talk to the science folks involved,

a, a disease like Xylella, these things are invisible. So what happens is someone says, "Well, I've got these stalks." They don't have them. "We've been to the farm." They, they might show up. We, I, I saw... I was in the Plant Health Lab the other day, some of these things show up 18 months into their growth. And so, uh, it's very, um, it's not an easy thing- Mm-hmm. ... uh, to manage this balance between...

And, and if you look back on the PSA thing, you know, we were trying to assist the kiwifruit sector and an importer to bring pollen into New Zealand. We thought we'd done all of the right checks and we thought it was okay. That turned out not to be, uh, and, and the cost of that then becomes vast. And so, I think we're just trying to get, we are trying to get that balance a, bit, a bit more right than where it has been and to try and speed up the process without compromising. One, one thing that we're quite keen to, to get, to express to people is

they can share these glasshouses for a lot of varieties. You don't have to go out on your own. There are ways to team up to reduce the cost as an importer. Hm. Um, so y- you know, I think, I think we have to help people with a bit of that knowledge as well. Right. Uh, Jo? Uh, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you, Ray.

Nice to see you and the team this morning. Yeah. I just want to acknowledge, uh, you and your team for all the work that you do supporting our rural communities and farmers. And, you know, I've got to know a few of you particularly through adverse weather events and what have you, so, um, I just thought it was important to acknowledge you and the team for all the work that you do. I want to talk about money now.

Money. Money, um, makes the world go round. Um, I see that there was, um, an underspend of, uh, quite a substantial amount. So you had an estimated expenditure of 906 million, but seems you've only spent 800 million. Now, I note further in your annual report, you've talked about being able to get approval to retain 17 million of underspend towards biosecurity. So, given the current climate with biosecurity and, and the hornet incursion and- Yup.

... other potential incursions-Are you, uh, you also mentioned, sorry, I'm kind of thinking as, as I'm thinking.
